data.title: Capricor Therapeutics: Shares Soaring After DMD Study Win - Ball Now In FDA's Court
data.description:
Investment Overview - Capricor's Stock Soars On Positive Study Results

The share price of San Diego based biotech Capricor Therapeutics, Inc. (CAPR) is exploding upwards today - up >450% at the time of writing, trading at a value of $37, with a market cap valuation of ~$1.6bn.

The gains are being driven by the company's announcement of positive results from its HOPE 3 study of lead candidate deramiocel, indicated to treat the devastating muscle-wasting disease Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy ("DMD").

Deramiocel demonstrated statistically significant improvements in both skeletal and cardiac function - according to a press release:

HOPE-3 is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, Phase 3 clinical trial evaluating Deramiocel in boys and young men with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

The study randomized 106 participants across 20 leading U.S. clinical sites. Participants received intravenous Deramiocel at 150 million cells per infusion or placebo every three months for a 12-month period.

The average age of participants was approximately 15 years, and all were on a stable corticosteroid regimen throughout the study.

Efficacy results were shared as follows:

The press release also shares comments from study investigator Craig McDonald, M.D., Distinguished Professor of Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation and Pediatrics at UC Davis Health, as follows:

A nearly 54 percent slowing of skeletal muscle disease progression is extraordinary in Duchenne and directly linked to maintaining independence and quality of life in the most severely affected patients with greatest unmet need.

The preservation of function reflected in PUL v2.0 translates into real, practical benefits for boys and young men living with this disease, and the effect of Deramiocel on cardiomyopathy will potentially translate to improved long-term survival.

The HOPE-3 study is the first-ever Phase 3 trial in a largely non-ambulatory population with DMD to successfully meet its primary endpoint and to support the development of an innovative therapy over many years with this level of impact has been a profound privilege.

Analysis - DMD Patients Difficult Year Ending On A High Note

The news will doubtless be welcomed by DMD patients and their care-givers, who have endured a difficult year, as a gene therapy developed by Sarepta Therapeutics, Elevidys, promising best-in-class efficacy, was associated with two patient deaths, leading to a Black Box safety warning for ambulatory patients, and the withdrawal of approval for non-ambulatory patients.

Sarepta also recently reported that two of its exon-skipping therapies for DMD, AMONDYS 45 and VYONDYS 53, granted accelerated approval (approval based on a biomarker indicative of treatment benefit, but is not itself a measure of clinical benefit) a decade ago, had failed to meet efficacy endpoints in a post-confirmatory study.

Earlier this year, the FDA had also declined to approve deramiocel, after Capricor submitted a Biologics License Application ("BLA"), requesting approval to market and sell its lead drug candidate.

According to a Capricor press release from September 2024

  • The BLA filing will be based on existing cardiac data from the Phase 2 HOPE-2 and HOPE-2 Open Label Extension (OLE) trials compared to natural history data provided by Vanderbilt University Medical Center and Cincinnati Children’s Hospital Medical Center.
  • In order to support potential label expansion to treat DMD skeletal muscle myopathy, Capricor plans to combine Cohorts A and B of the Phase 3 HOPE-3 clinical trial to serve as a post-approval study and does not intend to unblind Cohort A at this time, which was expected to occur in the fourth quarter of 2024.

In July this year, however, the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter ("CRL"), outlining its reasons for rejecting deramiocel for approval, causing Capricor stock to fall from ~$11, to ~$6. According to a Capricor press release:

In the CRL, the FDA stated that it had completed its review of the application but is unable to approve the BLA in its current form, specifically citing that the BLA does not meet the statutory requirement for substantial evidence of effectiveness and the need for additional clinical data. The CRL also referenced certain outstanding items in the Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls ("CMC") section of the application, most of which Capricor believes it has addressed in prior communications to the FDA.

Given that dermiocel had been granted a priority review by the FDA (shortening the length of its review period), and that it had opted against convening an Advisory Committee ("AdCom") to discuss the drug's approval case with a panel of industry experts, the news of the rejection must have come as a shock.

The HOPE-3 study is apparently "the first-ever Phase 3 trial in a largely non-ambulatory population with DMD to successfully meet its primary endpoint," and Capricor also states that it now:

plans to submit its response to the Complete Response Letter incorporating HOPE-3 data, following prior alignment with FDA.

Is Deramiocel Approval Now Guaranteed?

I don't think, even after today's news and Wall Street's reaction, that we may conclude approval is now a formality, but it is tempting to wonder if the FDA would have rejected the drug had this data been available at the time?

The drug was developed using Capricor's StealthX exosomes platform, a description of which is shown below (slide taken from a recent company presentation).

According to Capricor's Q3 2025 10Q filing, deramiocel is:

an allogeneic cell therapy comprised of cardiosphere-derived cells (“CDCs”), for the treatment of cardiomyopathy associated with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

CDCs are a unique population of cardiac cells derived from healthy donor hearts, and they exert their therapeutic effect through immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, pro-angiogenic, and anti-fibrotic mechanisms—primarily mediated by secreted exosomes enriched with bioactive microRNAs.

These non-coding RNA species influence gene expression in immune and tissue-resident cells, promoting regeneration and reducing inflammation, in contrast to dystrophin-restoring approaches like exon-skipping or gene therapy.

Capricor has developed a scalable, reproducible process for exosome purification, and has a manufacturing facility in La Jolla, California - the manufacturing process is shown below:

There are numerous biotechs attempting to guide DMD therapies through the clinical study and regulatory process - Dyne Therapeutics (DYN), Solid Biosciences (SLDB), Entrada Therapeutics (TRDA), for example, and several companies marketing and selling DMD drugs - Sarepta, as mentioned, PTC Therapeutics (PTCT), and Nippon Shinyaku ("NS") Pharma, which markets and sells Vitelspo.

Vitelspo had received an accelerated approval from the FDA, however a recent post-confirmatory study failed to meet endpoints, and Translarna, a PTC Therapeutics drug, faces withdrawal from the market in Europe after its marketing authorization was not renewed.

This - and Sarepta's struggles illustrate how difficult it is to develop safe and effective drug to treat DMD a disease described by the Muscular Dystrophy Association ("MDA") as follows:

Duchenne muscular dystrophy ("DMD") is a progressive, genetic (X-linked recessive) neuromuscular disorder caused by mutations to the DMD gene, resulting in the dysfunction or absence of the dystrophin protein. When functional, dystrophin helps keep muscle cells intact, and without dystrophin, muscle cells are vulnerable to injury, resulting in progressive muscle degeneration and weakness.

Deramiocel has a unique and differentiated mechanism of action ("MoA") to other therapies and candidates, and the endpoints for Capricor's Phase 3 study were agreed beforehand with the FDA, therefore it seems highly unlikely the administration would push back on efficacy, given the statistical significance of the results. Furthermore, the company notes in its latest 10-Q:

Importantly, safety data from the trial have been regularly reported to the FDA, and no significant safety signals have been observed. Across our programs, we have administered more than 800 infusions to approximately 150 DMD patients with Deramiocel continuing to demonstrate a strong and consistent safety profile.

It may be unclear at this point whether the FDA can fast-track an approval of deramiocel, or if Capricor will have to start the approval process over, which means its drug would likely not be approved and on the market for at least another 12 months.

Nevertheless, the market opportunity is not in doubt. Earlier this year, prior to its issues with Elevidys, Sarepta was guiding for ~$3bn of revenues from its DMD franchise, while Capricor has shared its own market research, shown below:

Concluding Thoughts - What's The Play On Capricor Stock Today?

Unfortunately, the ideal time to buy Capricor stock was of course yesterday. Shares traded <$5, but are now trading at their highest ever value - even higher that when Capricor stock jumped ~450% in value in September 2024, when the company first shared news that it would submit a BLA.

While the path to approval now looks far clearer than it did in September 2024, thanks to today's data, it is usually wise to be cautious until an approval has been officially secured.

For example, earlier this year the share price of UniQure (QURE) jumped from ~$10, to ~$70, as its Huntington's therapy succeeded in a pivotal clinical study, only for the FDA to then state it would not consider natural history data as a satisfactory comparator arm, despite prior agreement. Qure stock fell back <$25 on the news.

I am not suggesting something similar will happen with Capricor, but there have been many false dawns with DMD therapies in the past, so it may be wise not to take anything for granted.

Of course, today's news will be welcomed by the global DMD community, and by Capricor's management and its investors, and I wonder if Capricor may now become an acquisition target for a large Pharma company, given deramiocel now looks like a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) product in waiting. If a Pharma were to bid for the company, I'd expect a price tag of $5bn would by no means flatter Carpricor.

Sadly I don't hold Capricor stock, but I did hold some QURE stock, selling some and holding some after the company's study news broke. I would likely adopt a similar strategy with Capricor, noting that shares may continue to rise, as was the case with QURE, but also that any unexpected setback could result in a 50% stock price "haircut," and in the filed of biotech investing, it is always best to expect the unexpected.

Nevertheless, this may be a great day for DMD patients and care-givers, and soon the ball will be back in the FDA's court - perhaps we may see deramiocel on the market within 12 months, after all. I am issuing a Hold rating today, but I'd advise adding this company to your watchlists, as if deramiocel makes it to market, I would not be too surprised to see Capricor stock double in value. The risk reward remains highly intriguing, even after today's surge in the share price.

data.title: Buy Verizon, December's Ideal "Safer" Dog Of The Dow
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Dow Industrials is too pricey and reveals only skinny dividends, December (again) finds only Verizon (VZ), living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price and it is also ‘safer’ because it has more-than-enough free-cash-flow to support its dividends.

In late February, 2024 Dow Jones replaced the low priced high yield dividend payer Walgreens, with Amazon, a high-priced non-dividend payer. More recently, in November, 2024, Intel was replaced by Nvidia and Dow Inc was supplanted by Sherwin Williams

With renewed downside market pressure up to 84.4% all ten of the top ‘safer’ December Dow dividend dogs could become elite fair-priced buys with annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced Dow Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the mid-point of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 21.38% To 40.50% Net Gains From Top-Ten Dow Dogs By December 2026

Three of ten top dividend-yielding Dow dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this December, 2026 yield-based forecast for Dow dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 30% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1,000.00 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding Dow stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to December, 2026 were:

Salesforce (CRM) was projected to net $405.04, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 55 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 25% over the market as a whole.

NVIDIA (NVDA) was projected to net $383.62, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 64 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 129% greater than the market as a whole.

Boeing (BA) was projected to net $30395, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 6 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 18% greater than the market as a whole.

Nike (NKE) was projected to net $288.98, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 40 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% over the market as a whole.

Honeywell (HON) was projected to net $285.66 based on the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 2% under the market as a whole.

Microsoft (MSFT) was projected to net $282.35, based on the median of target price estimates from 56 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 7% greater than the market as a whole.

Amazon (AMZN) was projected to net $251.33, based on the median of target price estimates from 67 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 37% more than the market as a whole.

Disney (DIS) was projected to net $245.03, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 31 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 50% above the market as a whole.

Verizon was projected to net $223.68, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 67% Under the market as a whole.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) was projected to net $213.79 based on the median of target prices estimated by 28 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 57% under to the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 28.83% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Dow Index stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 17% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

30 Dow Dogs By 1Yr Total Returns

30 Dow Dogs By Analyst Target Gain Estimates

The December, 2025 Dow 30 By Yield

No December, 2025 Dow 30 Dogs Made the Top Ten in All Three Ranks: Returns, Targets, & Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top November Dow Dividend Stocks By Yield Ranged 2.48% To 6.95%

Top ten Dow Dogs represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

The lone communication services dog placed first, Verizon [1], to lead the pack. Then, in second place was another loner; this one from the energy sector, Chevron (CVX)[2].

Four healthcare stocks placed third, fourth, seventh, and eighth: Merck (MRK) [3], Amgen (AMGN) [4], then Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) [7], and United Health (UNH) [8].

The two October consumer staple stalwarts, placed fifth and sixth, Coca Cola Co (KO) [5], and Procter& Gamble (PG) [6].

Finally, two consumer discretionary members placed ninth and tenth, McDonald’s (MCD) [9], and Nike [10], to complete the November top-ten Dogs of the Dow by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graphic above shows the relative strengths of the top ten November Dow dogs by dividend and price as of market close 12/1/2025.

Nearly all top-ten Dow dogs show a less than ideal status because the prices of nine exceed the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by just one of the five lowest priced Dogs of the Dow. Only Verizon, lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

Dow Index Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 12/1/25

The above chart reveals thirty Dow dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

Two stocks in the Dow index pay no dividend and are, therefore, excluded from the calculation. For those that count, December’s least expensive dog of the Dow is Verizon at $14.76 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is NVIDIA (NVDA) at $4,504.50 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Two of Twenty-Eight Dow Dividend Dogs Show No or Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

No or a negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividend, The December pair are Coca-Cola, and 3M. They are considered unsafe to buy due to dividend yields equaling or exceeding free cash flow yields.

Two cut their dividends in the 2020 Ides of March depression times, Boeing, and The Walt Disney Co. One more Salesforce , snuck onto the Dow index without a dividend.

However, in March 2023, Salesforce declared its first-ever quarterly payout and Disney, in January, 2024 rejoined the dividend paying ranks. However four (two short on cash and two non-dividend payers, Amazon, and Boeing) cast a pall on the Dow by being stockholder adverse.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, TACO deals, wars, ICE, and vigilante actions, too.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Rerates Could Get All Ten Dow Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, one in the top ten is already ideally priced. Beside Verizon, already in the ideal zone, nine more top-yield stocks are within $261.42 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or split shares. Those options may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 19.37% To 40.79% Top Ten Dow Index Upsides To December, 2026; (32), Six -0.48 to -4.44% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 26.02% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Dow Dogs To December, 2026

Ten-top Dow December-updated yield (dividend / price) results, as verified by YCharts, made the following ranking:

As noted-above, top-ten Dow dogs selected 12/1/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. (Real Estate is not reported by the Dow and Utilities has its own Dow Index. Materials, Financials, Industrials, and Technology also missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield Dow Dogs (34) To Deliver 17.09% Vs. (35) 13.56% Net Gains by All Ten Come December, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dow Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 8.63% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fifth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Procter & Gamble, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 30.44%.

The five lowest-priced Dow top-yield dogs for December were: Verizon; Nike; Cola-Cola; Merck; Procter & Gamble, with prices ranging from $40.74 to $147.44.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of December 1 were: Chevron; Johnson & Johnson; UnitedHealth; Amgen; Home Depot (HD), with prices ranging from $152.54 to $357.33.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the feckless Fed and Trade war-fueled slide gains momentum into 2026, with the exception of a very few Techno, and (maybe) Energy stocks, look for at least half of the ten Dow Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or quite suddenly.

Afterword

Buy One ‘Safer' Dow December DiviDog and Watch Four More

Late December, 2025, 22 of the 30 Dow Index were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 12/1/25 data showed one was fair-priced.

How Dow 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price” Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that one dividend from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Dow dogs conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share. That one is Verizon Communications still standing after passing the ‘safer’ dividend gauntlet.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of 84.4% is required to put all 10 “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or create stock splits to bring prices the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dogs of the Dow updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dow dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations. Just remember Verizon In December 2025 and the five stand-by Dow dogs of the month: Cisco (CSCO), Chevron, Procter & Gamble, Honeywell, and McDonald’s.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: November ADP: ADP Signals Clear Labor Market Softening Ahead Of The Fed's December Decision
data.description:
The November ADP report arrives at a moment when the labor market narrative is finally breaking from the steady but muted pattern that defined most of the year. Hiring demand has been cooling for several months, and the latest reading reinforces a shift toward clearer signs of softening across industries and firm sizes. What makes this release especially important is its timing: with no official November payroll report available, the ADP data effectively serves as one of the last meaningful signals the Fed will see before its December meeting. As a result, today’s update carries more interpretive weight than usual, helping shape the broader conversation around labor market momentum and the policy response that may follow.

ADP Employment Data

ADP estimated in the November edition of its National Employment Report that private payrolls declined by -32,000, the largest monthly decline that ADP has reported since March 2023. The fourth monthly decline in six months was well below the modestly optimistic forecast of an increase of 10,000. The weak reading was further confirmation of a trend of deterioration in hiring demand that has been developing since Q2 2025. The three-month average dropped to below zero for the first time since the pandemic, falling from around 37,000 in July to 5,000 in October and now around -4,300 in November.

The details point to a broad-based decline across industries, with education & health services continuing to be an outlier:

  • The private goods sector saw employment down -19,000 in November, led by declines in construction (-9,000) and manufacturing (-18,000). The manufacturing industry specifically has seen four straight months of declines, with the November drop being the largest in that sequence, for a total decline in private manufacturing employment of -30,000 in that period.
  • The private services sector saw employment decline -13,000 in November. Within the services industries, the changes were more mixed, but the biggest declines were seen in information (-20,000) and professional & business services (-26,000). Offsetting the job losses in the rest of the sector were gains in education & health services (+33,000) and leisure & hospitality (+13,000). Excluding education & health services, the headline employment change would have been the worst since the pandemic at -65,000.

The segmentation by firm size shows that the decline in employment continues to be concentrated in small businesses. ADP reports that smaller firms lost a total of -120,000 jobs in November, while mid-sized and large firms both saw job gains of 51,000 and 39,000, respectively. This is a continuation of the trend we have seen over the last few months and confirms that the labor market is experiencing a cyclical downtrend where the economically sensitive parts of the economy are hurting the most.

ADP’s wage data also signaled weakness in the labor market. The annual pay growth of job changers has been tracked as an indicator of tightness in employment data, and it just saw its largest deceleration since September 2024, which, coincidentally, was when the Fed decided to cut rates by 50 bps. Job changers' pay growth now sits at 6.3% YoY in November, falling from 6.7% YoY in October, the lowest reading since February 2021. The wage data also support the narrative that small businesses are leading the labor market deterioration. While large and mid-sized firms' wage growth (for job stayers) was between 4.7% and 4.9% YoY, wage growth in the smallest segment of firm size (1-19 employees) was just 2.5% YoY.

Beige Book Confirmed

The ADP employment data today confirms the last piece of major evidence that the Fed would consider in its December meeting (since it won’t have a November jobs report to analyze), the Beige Book. In the November 2025 edition of the Beige Book, the broad qualitative view of employment shifted from “largely stable but muted demand” to “employment declined slightly,” a clear shift in the language used to describe the situation. In tune with that shift, we see the ADP employment gain move from a 47,000 increase in October to a -32,000 decline in November. Importantly, we won’t get a BLS number that contradicts either of these key views of the labor market, so for me, the case for a December rate cut is rock solid.

Market Reaction

Financial markets have been moving in a dovish direction ever since the NY Fed's Williams gave a speech that appeared to support a rate cut, leading markets to believe that the expected vote count in December would be for a cut. For that reason, the reaction to the ADP data was not substantial.

  • US equity futures were basically unchanged after the release of the ADP report before starting to decline more substantially around 9 am. The S&P 500 opened in the red but has settled at a slight gain of 0.1% an hour after the open. A more interesting move correlated with rate expectations can be seen in the Russell 2000 index, which is up 0.8% after an hour of trading.

  • Bond yields have been trading lower since mid-November as rate cut expectations picked up, and the reaction to the ADP data was limited. The 2-year and 10-year yields both saw declines immediately after the data came out, but the moves were both reversed. The yield curve is still broadly lower today, and notably, the 1-month (orange) and 3-month (blue) rates are both making new multiyear lows.
  • The USD had a choppy morning, and a slight decline after the ADP release was reversed in the hour after. Throughout it all, the US dollar index (DXY) performance is moving with the yield curve, down about -0.3% around 10:30 am.

The FedWatch probabilities have actually not moved much in reaction to the November ADP report. Post-release, the probability of a cut is 89%, only slightly higher than 88% yesterday and 83.4% a week ago. Still, the broader context has shifted enough for me to reassess my own view. About a month ago, I called that the Fed would pause due to a lack of data, but that was the wrong assessment. I overestimated how much the lack of data from the government shutdown and the surrounding uncertainty would shape the Fed’s thinking, and the incoming information has been more definitive than I expected. With a week to go until the announcement, I am now shifting to the expectation that the Fed will cut by 25 bps in the December meeting. Some expect a close vote based on speeches in November, but I don’t expect there to be a significant number of dissenters (2-3 at most) due to the fact that the Beige Book and ADP data have been very convincing in describing a deteriorating labor market.

data.title: The Main Threat For 2026
data.description:
What all bubbles have in common

During an interview with CNBC, the famous investor Ray Dalio explained his concerns about a potential stock market bubble. He believes that the AI enthusiasm is going too far, but at the same time, a potential crash might not happen soon.

The reason is that every bubble has the same trigger: a tightening of monetary policy, and we are heading toward the opposite side. As long as the Fed keeps cutting rates, AI companies get access to cheaper capital, and this is definitely a bullish signal.

If we go back in history, the tightening of monetary policy was the main reason for the bubble to pop.

The tech bubble was born when borrowing was relatively cheap and then continued its rise when the Fed Funds Rate was stable at 4-5%. Companies understood that the Internet was revolutionary and started to invest an increasing amount of money that ultimately impacted the GDP. A rising GDP, higher economic activity, and stable interest rates inflated the bubble even more.

However, inflation started to push higher, and the Fed decided to set the interest rates gradually above 6%. This maneuver dampened the enthusiasm as companies were less willing to borrow, and this triggered a vicious cycle.

The same happened with the Great Financial Crisis.

The seeds of the crisis were sown after the bursting of the tech bubble. To stimulate the economy, the Fed reduced interest rates significantly, deregulation took place, and everyone could get access to cheap loans/mortgages. The hype lasted until the inflation issue stepped in, and the Fed started to raise interest rates gradually. In early 2007 it was no longer so convenient to invest in real estate; the installments were way higher, and this led to a sharp housing market crash.

The history repeated itself a couple of years ago, when everyone was so euphoric about the renewable energy trend/EV and tech stocks.

Once again, inflation joined the chat, and the Fed was forced to raise interest rates. This time, the stock market began its decline even before the Fed Funds Rate topped.

Overall, a tightening monetary policy is the biggest enemy for stock market bubbles. The good news is that we are heading in the opposite direction; in fact, the market is pricing in 3-4 cuts of 25 bps each until December 2026.

However, we know how unpredictable the economy is over the short term, and the Fed’s choices sometimes are forced. The primary intention is to cut rates to support the deteriorating labor market, but we can’t take the inflation issue completely out of the equation.

The market is currently pricing in just a 0.50% probability that the Fed Funds Rate remains the same (3.75%-4%) until December 2026, let alone a potential rise. In other words, everyone is certain that 2026 will be characterized by an easing monetary policy. Therefore, I believe the biggest threat for 2026 is that this scenario turns out to be wrong.

Just to be clear, I don’t think this should be the base case, but probably it shouldn’t be completely overlooked as the market is currently doing. If inflation gets higher, the Fed can’t lower interest rates anymore, and in this article I’ll show you why I think this scenario deserves a percentage of occurrence higher than 0%-0.50%.

Nothing points to a decline in inflation

First of all, the current inflation rate is not low. The Fed’s target is 2%, and the last core PCE index was 2.90%, so we are almost 50% higher than we should be. An inflation rate a bit lower than 3% is definitely not the end of the world, but I invite you to reflect on a few key aspects.

What is the Fed doing to prevent a further increase? What is the government doing to keep inflation low?

The monetary policy points in the opposite direction

Yesterday, the Federal Reserve officially ended the quantitative tightening of its balance sheet: from early 2022, it has reduced its assets by approximately $2.40 trillion.

The huge balance sheet expansion during COVID-19 has been partially offset, but we already have signs of a new quantitative easing in 2026.

The banking system is experiencing liquidity headwinds; therefore, the Fed started to inject liquidity through overnight repurchase agreements. In particular, the last operation concerned $13.50 billion, the second largest since the COVID-19 period.

The Fed seems willing to print money as soon as the first cracks arise, and I expect many more liquidity injections throughout 2026. The stock market is addicted to liquidity pumps, so this is seen as positive news for investors. However, such a procyclical approach has its limits if the inflation rate is already above the target. QE and money printing should be considered as non-conventional monetary policy tools, but they are actually becoming the norm.

Furthermore, the market expects with 87% probability a 25-bps rate cut in the next FOMC meeting and 2-3 more up until December 2026. In all this, we have a huge wildcard over the next days.

President Trump announced that he has already picked the next Fed Chair, and it will probably be someone with a similar vision regarding the monetary policy. The problem is that President Trump believes that the Fed Funds Rate should be cut by 300 bps. Such a big rate cut is very unlikely, but I wouldn’t rule out a new Chairman more prone than Powell to cut interest rates.

From whatever side we look at it, the 2026 monetary policy seems more expansionary than 2025, so I wonder why inflation should come down. The economic machine, even though not perfect, tends to be pretty straightforward: if you lower rates significantly, the inflation rate is subject to upward pressure.

Fiscal policy is anything but restrictive

If monetary policy is dovish, a restrictive fiscal policy might offset the inflationary upward pressure, but this is not happening. Quite the opposite.

Since 2020, the market has become addicted to huge fiscal deficits and now seems very hard to break below the $1 trillion threshold. The forecast for FY2026 talks about a fiscal deficit between $1.70 trillion and $2.20 trillion, therefore at least equal to FY2025 but potentially higher. The One Big Beautiful Bill played a significant role in this, and we don’t know yet if the government is serious about the $2,000 check to each US person.

Besides the inflationary nature of the increasing fiscal deficits, the US interest rates (on the long end of the yield curve) might experience a sharp rise due to debt sustainability concerns, regardless of the Fed's monetary policy:

We spent $4 trillion on interest over the last decade, but will spend $14 trillion in the next ten years. Interest costs crowd out important public and private investments in our future, harming the economy for every American.

Michael A. Peterson, CEO of the Peter G. Peterson Foundation.

As in the case for monetary policy, whatever you want to look at, the fiscal policy for 2026 seems anything but restrictive. But inflationary pressures don’t end here, as tariffs are starting to push imported goods prices higher. That is why the 40% tariff against Brazil was lifted a couple of weeks ago (prices of coffee, beef, fruits, and cocoa were skyrocketing).

Conclusion

Bubble or not, as long as the inflation rate remains ~3%, I do think the stock market has enough momentum to keep rising. The situation changes completely when the inflation rate gets higher, since the Fed would be forced to raise the interest rates. As pointed out by Ray Dalio, in the past this meant the end of the ride.

So, the $1 million question is, will the inflation rate rise significantly in 2026? We’ll see, but the market seems rather skeptical about it, even though three forces are threatening higher prices in 2026: easing monetary policy, massive fiscal deficit, and tariffs.

The parallel with 1966-1984 is very interesting, but there is one crucial factor that is still missing: oil prices. If the latter starts to ramp up, the main threat for 2026 would materialize.

As of now, oil prices are very depressed, and nobody seems interested anymore in the black gold; however, I want to conclude this article with further food for thought. Data centers are mainly powered by natural gas, but what if oil starts having a role in this? After all, the US hyperscalers are building data centers as big as cities, and they will need a massive amount of power to be constructed and work properly.

data.title: BCP Investment Corporation: Best Opportunity After A Deep Dive Into Private Credit
data.description:
This article dives deep into issues affecting all Private Credit options in recent months. I discuss the specific hazards these months have identified in great detail because those hazards are relevant in contrasting ways to different specific vehicles. I also discuss these vehicles in more detail than probably typical to secondary ticker on Seeking Alpha. And there is a reason for that. While FS Specialty Lending (OTCPK:FSSL) which began trading November 13th and may represent the most complicated CEF observation in my lifetime, its "opportunity" to buy at a meaningful discount is most relevant only in pointing to a clearer opportunity. Similarly, the newfound discount in Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), is hard to see as a justifiable buy when an opportunity fundamentally separated from OBDC's recent headwind is comparatively a superior value even before NAV accretion.

My favorite exposure to private credit is BCP Investment Corp (BCIC), trading at a roughly 26% discount to NAV and benefitting from several shareholder friendly initiatives. But before covering BCIC and its contrast from recent Private Credit issues, I need to dive deep into issues shrouding much of private credit.

What happened in Private Credit?

We have been inundated with talking heads covering private credit since September. This section may serve those frustrated by the lack of grit or and clear context in that coverage.

You already know that Tri Color Holdings and First Brands filed for bankruptcy. Those filings were September 10th and September 28th respectively. But fewer know that liquid BDCs were correcting long before JP Morgan (JPM) took a $170 million charge on October 14th. That charge was more readily associated with the credit choice of Tri Color, but I'm going to ask a sarcastic question: Didn’t we all love how Jamie Dimon’s simultaneous “cockroaches” analogy initially punished everybody else for the portfolio choices JP Morgan had made? Jeffries (JEF) and Fifth Third (FITB) certainly got crawled on in the First Brands and TriColor bankruptcies respectively.

Looking at Private Credit focused vehicles, the vehicle now trading as FSSL is rather unique among Private Credit peers for having been invested in the likes of First Brands. While this article will continue to address the complicated nature of the FSSL’s risk/reward opportunity in the next section, I feel it is far too early to rate FSSL.

Most publicly traded BDCs inclusive of BCIC and OBDC have been able to discuss avoiding such problems on their quarterly calls. And since the generic “cockroaches” analogy, we got some clarity and name-naming. It appears that focused Private Credit Advisors’ talent at BCIC (Mount Logan, MLIC), and OBDC (Blue Owl, OWL) for avoiding such exposure exceeds that of the banks.

But portfolio risk is only half the risk and half the story with Private Credit...

The unheralded second cause of unusually high discounts in Private Credit

Separate from default headlines, efforts at certain Advisors to maximize “permanent capital” and/or keep that capital captive at all costs for the highest possible Assets Under Management (“AUM”) fees tends to create a pent up desire for selling across the Private Credit space. Obviously, all advisors and definitely Private Credit advisors prefer growing AUM but ideally the demand for their investment vehicles tends to benefit from finding a middle ground – enhancing returns with NAV accretive actions like tender offers. Maintaining and maximizing attractive distributions is especially important.

I only invest directly in BDCs which are publicly traded and at opportune times. But a historical context of FSSLs legacy shareholders may best demonstrate the effect of unwinding any inability to sell. This unwinding is the clearest explanation of FSSL's current 20%+ discount as a similar unwinding was observed driving sister fund FSCO's discount worse than 30% in 2023. (see "Pricing Information" tab, 5yr)

FSSL is more relevant than FSCO today because legacy shareholders have a new opportunity to sell (at a discount) for the first time in a long time. Those legacy shareholders come from a non-traded vehicle called FS Energy and Power Fund. While the name and the industry concentration within Private Credit have since changed, the legacy vehicle decided to terminate the company’s quarterly tender offer and suspend the share repurchase program way back in 2020. If these legacy investors are truly displeased, I would expect the discount to get wider than 30% at some point in time. So far, any selling pressure has been absorbed better than I expected.

Non-traded vehicles similar to FSSL predecessor can affect supply and demand mechanics for traded vehicles -- particularly when the size of an investor base lacking for liquidity is large.

Few among us realize that what really started the private credit selldown was a small distribution cut that unfortunately occurred at an absolutely massive Private Credit fund. On September 18, Blackstone's massive Blackstone Private Credit Fund (BCRED) announced a new lower monthly distribution. I do not expect Blackstone to talk openly about the challenges of growing what I perceive as a mature fund. But importantly, BCRED had first hit its 5% quarterly redemptions limit in December of 2022. To a lessor extent than the legacy FSSL shareholders, liquidity had already long been insufficient for redemptions at BCRED. So, $75 Billion in shareholders at BCRED reacting to their lower distributions have been so limited to consider exiting different BDCs instead – those that were publicly traded inclusive of BCIC and OBDC.

OWL, which I had still been seeing as an earlier life-cycle Private Credit advisor, was in November trying to merge semi-permanent capital Blue Owl Capital Corporation II into a permanent capital vehicle, Blue Owl Capital Corporation I (OBDC). Importantly, this was at a time when OBDC’s liquid market price was meaningfully below its net asset value. Blue Owl did move away from that plan for at least the short term. But the Private Credit space saw selling pressure again, and Blue Owl may experience longer lasting effects. OBDC trades at a roughly 10% discount today.

BCP Investment Corp turns the adverse trends in the Private Credit space on their head

Longer term, larger than 20% NAV discounts like FSSL (and BCIC) are scarce, even among publicly traded 1940 Act Funds focused on income. But things can get quite messy before they get better, and I do fear that may be the case with FSSL, given the new opportunity legacy FSSL shareholders have to exit, and FS Specialty Lending Advisor having stepped into what most focused Private Credit advisors avoided.

For total return in the Private Credit space, I see one clearly superior alternative to both FSSL and OBDC which themselves are intriguing options. It is BCIC which looks like the far superior opportunity to benefit from the correction that took place among the publicly traded Private Credit vehicles in recent months. Here is why:

BCP Investment Corp. trades at a roughly 26% discount to its recently published $17.55 NAV. More importantly, BCIC has a forward looking tailwind highly lacking in the space at present. A tailwind that absolutely bucks the space's issues with captivity. BCIC has a first NAV accretive tender of what is expected to be 20% of its total shares in aggregate through July 2027 already underway. These tender offers provide excess liquidity at a time some are seeking to exit Private Credit. Non-selling shareholders benefit from NAV accretion and those who want to exit depart at whatever discount the dutch auction process determines.

In this first tender offer, shareholders can specify a price they would be willing to sell in 13 cent increments between $13.63 and $14.93. As of writing, the low end of BCIC’s dutch tender range represents a premium to market, and the high end of the range would still accrete NAV meaningfully by buying back shares at roughly eighty-five cents per $17.55 NAV dollar. It appears insiders are not tendering their shares. In fact, insiders will independently buy $1.4 million of shares alongside the NAV accretive buyback at the dutch auction price. If fewer than $9 million of BCIC shares are tendered anywhere in the $13.63-$14.93 range, the company gets the entire block of tendered stock for NAV accretion.

Buying back shares while trading at a discount to NAV sends an extremely positive message to shareholders and in this case the current Dutch Auction tender is only the first half of a plan to buy back 20% of shares by mid July of 2027.

My notes from a recent quarterly conference call included BCIC’s affirmation of their anticipation to maintain the distribution level through market cycles. At market, the current yield is 14.4% which benefits from the NAV discount. BCIC has also announced an intention to move toward more shareholder friendly monthly distributions in 2026. At market, the current yield is 14.4% which benefits from the NAV discount.

BCIC risks and Putting Private Credit in Perspective

This article has been heavily tilted toward the risks of Private Credit, including portfolio defaults and supply/demand imbalance. I do want to again highlight risks of exposure to bankruptcies and liquidity issues. While the market’s ebbs and flows are not my core competency, it certainly feels like prognosticators warning now about private credit in December are beyond late. That said, I'm often wrong, and there are much safer ways to beat CDs and Government bonds discussed in an expert tutorial on Seeking Alpha than accessing Private Credit. To be clear, I have chosen larger positions in preferreds protected by the 1940 Act than I have chosen in Private Credit inclusive of BCIC.

While BCIC has emerged as a very clean alternative among Private Credit opportunities in recent months, any future for BCIC is possible. And after mid-July 2027, there is no clarity whether BCIC will appear as shareholder friendly as it presently does. Volatility could also surround tenders between now and 2027.

I also want to be very transparent in this section that I can be interpreted as having a conflict of interest with regards to BCIC. I am also a shareholder of BCIC’s advisor, Mount Logan Capital Inc where I anticipate a tender offer meaningfully above current market price. I am not going to portray any belief that MLIC is appropriate to buy, nor sell as I do not believe to possess any professional competency for investing in operating companies broadly.

I aim to never have a bias for, nor against Private Credit. I believe my primary competency is 1940 Act Companies inclusive of Closed End Funds (“CEFs”) and Business Development Companies (“BDCs”). I see the common shares of these funds (including FSSL, FSCO, and BCIC in Private Credit) as portfolio tools with which I can efficiently allocate for whatever changing portfolio exposure I want in the Private Credit or elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EV Company News For The Month Of November 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the November 2025 edition of Electric Vehicle [EV] company news.

The past month saw a solid month for global EV sales reported for October. Europe plugin electric car sales are booming, China sales remain very strong (albeit the rate of growth slowed down somewhat), and US sales are terrible (following the Sept. 30 tax credit expiry).

We also had the 2025 Guangzhou Auto Show in late November featuring the latest EVs in China. You can view a great video here.

Plugin electric car sales as of end October 2025

Global plugin electric car sales were over 1.9 million in October 2025, up 10% on October 2024 sales. Global plugin electric car market share in October was 28% and 26% YTD (for 2025).

China plugin electric car sales were ~1.3m in October 2025, up 7% on October 2024 sales. Plugin electric car market share in China for October was 57%, and 53% YTD (for 2025).

Europe plugin electric car sales were 346,000 in October 2025, up 36% YoY, reaching 33% market share and 28% YTD (for 2025). Norway reached a record 98.4% share, Sweden 65.7%, Germany 33.3%, France 31.1%, and UK 37.6% in October 2025.

USA plugin electric car sales were 91,067 (as reported by Argonne National Laboratory) in October 2025, down 26.9% on October 2024 sales. Plugin electric car market share in USA for October was 7.16%.

Latin America market share is 6% in Q3 2025, with plugin electric car sales rising fast (65% YTD).

Australia plugin electric car sales market share was 12% for October 2025.

Also of interest is booming EV sales in 'Rest of World' ("ROW") (up 35% in October 2025); notably Vietnam (+117% this year), India (+98%), Indonesia (+101%), and Turkey (+157%).

Note: Globally, 100% battery electric cars represented ~66% market share YTD (not updated this month) of all plugin electric cars sold. The above sales include light commercial vehicles.

Note: An acknowledgement to CleanTechnica for their work compiling most of the electric car sales data quoted above and in the article below.

Top selling global plugin electric car groups YTD (as of end October 2025) (source)

Global EV sales forecasts in the years ahead

BloombergNEF are bullish on 2025 global plugin EV sales forecasting ~22 million sales and 30% YoY growth (source)

Note: You can read the BloombergNEF Electric Vehicle Outlook 2025 here.

EV Volumes forecasts global plugin electric car sales to reach 45% share & 46m sales in 2030 (source)(forecast as of early 2024)

The IEA state: "Global electric light-duty vehicle sales are set to reach 40% in 2030 and almost 55% in 2035 based on current policy settings."

On January 28, 2025 Reuters reported:

EV car sales to top 20 million in 2025, research firm says. Chinese brands are set to account for nearly two-thirds of all EV deliveries worldwide... research firm Rho Motion forecast on Tuesday.

Seeking Alpha's Trend Investing global electric plugin car sales forecast

Sales (million) Market share
2025 21.3 24%
2030 46 51%
2035 73 81%

Trend Investing comment

We are still only at the extremely early stage of the EV boom - We have still only electrified < 10% of the global fleet of 1.475 billion, with a staggering ~90%+ still to go.

Trend Investing's global plugin electric car sales forecast to 2040 (green bars)

EV market news

On November 14 CleanTechnica reported:

US plugin vehicle sales dropped 27% year over year in October...A total of 91,067 plug-in vehicles (74,897 BEVs and 16,170 PHEVs) were sold during October 2025 in the United States, down 26.9% from the sales in October 2024. PEVs captured 7.16% of total LDV sales this month...

Note: The US$7,500 EV tax credit finished on 30 September 2025, hence the drop in US EV sales in October.

On November 26 Automotive News Europe reported:

European automakers hope for reprieve on 2035 combustion-engine ban. Europe’s embattled automakers are hoping for a reprieve when Brussels unveils an auto sector package next month that could weaken an effective ban on new combustion engines initially slated for 2035.

On November 25 Seeking Alpha reported:

EU cars sales show rising market share for BYD, while Tesla numbers slump... For the first ten months of 2025, total EU car registrations grew 1.4% compared with the same period last year. In the battery electric vehicle (BEV) segment, registrations surged by 38.6% YTD, driven particularly by a massive 90.1% jump in Spain, 63.2% in France, 47.7% in Germany, and 27.4% in the Netherlands...YTD petrol car registrations declined by 18.3%...the diesel car market declined by 24.5%...

Note: Bold emphasis by the author.

On November 25 Bloomberg reported:

Europe car sales keep climbing as automakers tout budget EVs... Consumers are being lured by an increasing array of cheaper fully electric models such as Stellantis NV’s Citroën ë-C3 city car... European sales of electrified vehicles jumped in October, driven by a 40% increase from plug-in hybrids. Registrations of fully electric cars were up by almost a third...BYD Co.’s sales in the region more than tripled, as the Chinese automaker easily outstripped Tesla Inc., whose slump persisted, with registrations down 48%...Automakers will meet with European Union officials next month to discuss potential revisions to Brussels’ plan to ban the sale of new combustion-engine cars by 2035...European carmakers may be set for a recovery from next year, according to Bloomberg Intelligence, with new EV subsidies, cost measures and strategy overhauls improving the outlook for 2026 and 2027.

On November 25 TechCrunch reported:

China’s Pony AI plans to triple global robotaxi fleet by the end of 2026. The company, which has about 961 robotaxis in the fleet today, announced the goal during its third-quarter earnings. Pony.ai is targeting a 1,000-robotaxi vehicle fleet by the end of this year. Its goal is to “surpass” 3,000 vehicles by the end of 2026.

On November 26 CNBC reported:

Uber rolls out driverless robotaxis in Abu Dhabi...Uber users in Abu Dhabi can ride in a fully driverless WeRide robotaxi starting Wednesday. Uber has bet big on autonomous vehicle technology, partnering with Google’s Waymo and electric vehicle maker Lucid.

On November 27 Reuters reported:

Pentagon seeks to add Alibaba, Baidu, BYD to China military list, Bloomberg News reports. It is not immediately clear if the companies have been added to the Pentagon's 1260H list of Chinese companies deemed military-linked but operating in the U.S., Bloomberg reported. While the designation does not involve immediate bans, it can be a blow to the reputations of affected companies and represents a stark warning to U.S. entities and firms about the risks of conducting business with them.

On November 28 Seeking Alpha reported:

Chinese EV makers warn of a deliveries slowdown as incentives fade...After January 1, 2026, new energy vehicles (battery EVs, plug-in hybrids, and fuel-cell vehicles) in China lose their full exemption from the government's vehicle purchase tax and move to a 50% exemption, with the per-vehicle benefit capped at about RMB15,000 through 2027, from a prior level of RMB30,000.

Note: RMB 15,000 is ~USD 2,120. This may have a small impact on Q1 2026, but little to zero impact thereafter.

EV company news

BYD Co. [SHE: 002594][HK:1211](OTCPK:BYDDY), Yangwang, Denza (OTCPK:OTCPK:BYDDF)

BYD is currently ranked the number 1 globally with 22.2% market share YTD (as of end October 2025). BYD is ranked number 1 in China with 28.0% market share YTD (as of end October 2025).

On November 12 CNEVPOST reported:

Automakers' share of China NEV market in Oct: BYD leads with 23.1%, Tesla drops out of top 10.

  • Tesla's share of China's NEV market in October stood at 2.03 percent, dropping out of the top 10 for the first time since August 2022.
  • BYD's October share was lower than September's 26.7 percent and also below the 36.1 percent recorded in the same month last year.

On November 24 CNEVPOST reported:

BYD launches Yuan Up DM-i hybrid SUV in Europe instead of its home market in China. The BYD Yuan Up DM-i, rebranded as Atto 2 DM-i in Europe...The Yuan Up DM-i offers a battery range of up to 90 km, with a combined range of up to 1,000 km when fully fueled and charged.

On November 25 CNEVPOST reported:

BYD's Oct registrations in Europe jump 206.8%, while Tesla slips 48.5%. BYD's new vehicle registrations in Europe reached 17,470 units in October, while Tesla's dropped to 6,964 units.

Geely Automobile Holdings Ltd. (OTCPK:GELYY, HK:0175), Volvo Cars, Polestar (PSNY), Kandi Technologies Group (NASDAQ:KNDI), Proton, Lotus, Zeekr (ZK) (also owns Lynk), Farizon. (Note: Volvo Group is a separate company that makes e-trucks & e-buses)

Geely-Volvo is currently ranked number 2 in the global electric car manufacturer's sales ranking with 10.8% global market share YTD (as of end October 2025). Geely-Volvo is the number 2 ranked in China with 12.4% market share YTD (as of end October 2025). Geely-Volvo is 5th ranked in Europe with 7.7% market share YTD (as of end October 2025).

On November 2 Geely announced:

Geely Automobile Holdings Limited total sales volume for October 2025 was 307,133 units total sales volume up 35% yoy.

On November 17 CNEVPOST reported:

Geely posts 59% profit growth in Q3 on strong sales.

  • Geely Auto reported a net income of RMB 3.82 billion ($538 million) for the third quarter, marking a 59 percent year-on-year increase.
  • Geely delivered a record 761,009 vehicles in the third quarter, a 42.52 percent year-on-year increase.

Tesla Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA)

Tesla is currently ranked the number 3 globally with 7.8% global market YTD (as of end October 2025). Tesla is number 5 in China with 4.5% market share YTD (as of end October 2025). Tesla is no longer ranked in the top 5 in Europe (as of end October 2025). Tesla is the number 1 electric car seller in the U.S with 41% of sales in Q3 2025.

On November 23 Seeking Alpha reported:

Elon Musk aims to dominate AI chip production...“Our goal is to bring a new AI chip design to volume production every 12 months," he posted on X. "We expect to build chips at higher volumes ultimately than all other AI chips combined.” “These chips will profoundly change the world in positive ways, saving millions of lives due to safer driving and providing advanced medical care to all people via Optimus,” Musk said...The company’s current AI chip iteration, AI4, is already integrated into Tesla vehicles, with AI5 on the brink of deployment and work on AI6 already in progress.

Tesla to double Austin robotaxi fleet in December, Musk says...Tesla's robotaxi service currently operates in two cities, Austin and the San Francisco Bay Area, with safety monitors still required in the vehicles. The company last week also received a permit to operate a ride-hailing service in Arizona.

Volkswagen Group [Xetra:VOW](OTCPK:VWAGY) (OTCPK:VLKAF)/ Audi / Lamborghini/ Porsche (OTCPK:POAHF)/ Skoda/ Bentley/ AUDI (Audi/SAIC JV in China)

One year after its founding: Joint venture between Volkswagen Group and Rivian shows strong progress. Development of a state-of-the-art zonal architecture and software for future Volkswagen Group and Rivian models is progressing.
